stem: A 29-year-old G2P0 woman is reviewed in a tertiary early pregnancy assessment unit at 6 weeks and 4 days gestation by last menstrual period after referral from her general practitioner with mild right iliac fossa pain and intermittent light per-vaginal spotting for 48 hours. Her previous pregnancy was a first-trimester miscarriage; she has no history of pelvic infection, prior ectopic, tubal surgery, IUD or assisted reproduction. She is haemodynamically stable, afebrile, and has no shoulder-tip pain, dizziness or peritonism. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an empty uterus with no intrauterine gestational sac, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas, and an unruptured right adnexal mass measuring 28 mm containing a yolk sac but no foetal pole or cardiac activity. There is no haemoperitoneum. Her beta-hCG today is 3800 IU/L (yesterday 3500 IU/L), liver function tests, full blood count and renal function are normal, and she has no contraindications to methotrexate including no breastfeeding, no peptic ulcer disease, no significant pulmonary or hepatic disease. She lives 20 minutes from the hospital, has reliable transport, can attend for follow-up blood tests, and is keen to avoid surgery if reasonable. The obstetric registrar is considering single-dose intramuscular methotrexate as outpatient management and is checking each eligibility criterion against the RANZCOG and RCOG protocols.
lead_in: Which feature in this presentation, if changed, would most clearly contraindicate single-dose IM methotrexate as outpatient management?

options.text: An adnexal mass measuring 28 mm with a yolk sac present but no foetal cardiac activity
options.rationale: The RANZCOG/RCOG eligibility criteria include adnexal mass <35 mm and absence of foetal cardiac activity; her mass is within this size and there is no cardiac activity. This finding does not contraindicate methotrexate (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: A serum beta-hCG of 3800 IU/L with a 24-hour rise of 300 IU/L
options.rationale: Methotrexate eligibility usually requires beta-hCG <5000 IU/L (some units 1500 to 5000); 3800 is below threshold and a small rise is consistent with ectopic. This is within the eligible range (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Foetal cardiac activity demonstrated on the transvaginal ultrasound today within the right adnexal mass
options.rationale: The presence of foetal cardiac activity within the ectopic is an explicit contraindication to single-dose methotrexate in both RANZCOG and RCOG guidance because failure rates are much higher; salpingectomy or salpingostomy is the recommended management. Other absolute contraindications are haemodynamic instability, ruptured ectopic, significant haemoperitoneum, intrauterine pregnancy, breastfeeding, immunodeficiency, active hepatic or renal disease, blood dyscrasia, and inability to comply with follow-up (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Mild right iliac fossa tenderness without peritonism, fever or haemodynamic instability
options.rationale: Mild pain in a stable patient with no peritonism is not a contraindication and is common in tubal ectopic; only acute severe pain or peritonism would mandate surgery (RANZCOG).

options.text: Residence 20 minutes from the hospital with reliable transport and stated willingness to attend follow-up
options.rationale: Good access to care and willingness to follow up are pre-requisites for outpatient methotrexate and support, rather than contraindicate, this management option (RANZCOG).

stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 woman presents to an early pregnancy assessment unit with a positive urine pregnancy test, light per-vaginal spotting for 3 days and intermittent mild lower abdominal cramping. By last menstrual period she is approximately 6 weeks gestation but her cycles have been irregular over the last 12 months while she has been training for a half-marathon. She is haemodynamically stable, with no peritonism, shoulder-tip pain or dizziness. A high-quality transvaginal ultrasound performed by an experienced sonographer today shows an empty uterus with a thin endometrium, no intrauterine gestational sac, no adnexal mass, no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas and normal-appearing ovaries bilaterally. Her serum beta-hCG today is 1200 IU/L. She has no significant past medical or surgical history, no risk factors for ectopic pregnancy (no prior pelvic infection, IUD, tubal surgery or assisted reproductive technology), and no contraindications to methotrexate. She is well, has reliable transport and is willing to attend for repeat blood tests. The clinic registrar must decide whether to expectantly manage with serial hCG and rescanning above the discriminatory zone, image again immediately, treat empirically as ectopic with methotrexate, or reassure as presumed early viable intrauterine pregnancy below the imaging threshold.
lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 today as presumed ectopic pregnancy based on the empty uterus on transvaginal ultrasound
options.rationale: A pregnancy of unknown location with hCG below the transvaginal discriminatory zone (approximately 1500 to 2000 IU/L) and no ectopic seen on ultrasound is not yet diagnosed as ectopic; treating with methotrexate could terminate a viable early intrauterine pregnancy (RANZCOG).

options.text: Arrange diagnostic laparoscopy today for definitive diagnosis and possible salpingectomy because of the empty uterus and positive pregnancy test
options.rationale: Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for haemodynamic instability, suspected rupture or persistent diagnostic uncertainty after failed expectant management; it is not first line for PUL in a stable woman (RANZCOG).

options.text: Repeat the serum beta-hCG in 48 hours and rescan once it exceeds the discriminatory zone of 1500 to 2000 IU/L, with clear safety-netting
options.rationale: Pregnancy of unknown location in a stable woman with hCG below the discriminatory zone is managed with serial 48-hour hCG and rescanning once the hCG exceeds the threshold. A normal early intrauterine pregnancy doubles hCG every 48 to 72 hours; a fall >=50% suggests failing pregnancy; a plateau or sub-optimal rise increases ectopic concern. Safety-netting and ready re-presentation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Repeat the transvaginal ultrasound today by a second sonographer to confirm absence of an intrauterine pregnancy before any follow-up
options.rationale: A high-quality scan has already been performed; immediate repeat scanning is low-yield. Waiting for the hCG to cross the discriminatory zone is the standard approach (RANZCOG).

options.text: Reassure that this is a normal early intrauterine pregnancy and arrange routine antenatal booking for 8 to 10 weeks
options.rationale: Reassurance without exclusion of ectopic risks rupture; PUL must be followed up to a definitive diagnosis (IUP, failing PUL, ectopic) (RANZCOG).

stem: A 22-year-old G1P0 woman presents to her general practitioner at an estimated 11 weeks gestation by uncertain last menstrual period with three weeks of persistent nausea, intermittent vaginal spotting and one episode of passing what she describes as small grape-like tissue. She has no significant medical history. Examination shows a uterus palpable above the symphysis to a size consistent with 14 weeks, soft and non-tender. Blood pressure today is 138/86 mmHg (previously 110/70 at GP visit at 8 weeks). She has dry mucous membranes and reports mild postural symptoms. A transvaginal ultrasound at the local imaging service today shows a heterogeneous intrauterine mass with multiple small cystic spaces (described as 'snowstorm' appearance), no foetal pole, no foetal cardiac activity, and bilateral theca lutein cysts on the ovaries measuring 4 to 5 cm. The serum beta-hCG is 285 000 IU/L. Thyroid function tests show TSH 0.05 mU/L and free T4 22 pmol/L (mildly elevated). She has not been on any thyroid medication. She has been referred to the local gynaecology team and her GP wants to confirm the recognition and what the next steps are.
lead_in: Which sequence of management most accurately reflects current Australian (RANZCOG) guidance for this presentation?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 as outpatient with weekly hCG until undetectable, then 6-monthly surveillance for the following 12 months
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line for persistent gestational trophoblastic neoplasia, not for primary evacuation of a hydatidiform mole. Surgical evacuation is the primary treatment (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Suction curettage in theatre with histology, weekly hCG until three negative samples then monthly for 6 months (complete) or 1 month (partial), with reliable contraception
options.rationale: RANZCOG and the Australian/New Zealand Gestational Trophoblastic Disease group recommend suction curettage as the primary treatment for hydatidiform mole. Histopathology confirms complete (no foetus, diploid androgenetic) vs partial (foetus or foetal parts, triploid) mole. Surveillance: weekly beta-hCG until three consecutive negative weekly samples, then monthly for 6 months (complete mole) or until one month after normal hCG (partial). Reliable contraception is mandatory throughout because pregnancy obscures rising hCG that would indicate persistent GTN (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG only, given that many molar pregnancies will resolve spontaneously without surgical or medical intervention
options.rationale: Molar pregnancies do not reliably resolve spontaneously; surgical evacuation with histological confirmation is required to confirm the diagnosis and remove the trophoblastic tissue (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Total abdominal hysterectomy as primary treatment given the high reported risk of choriocarcinoma associated with complete hydatidiform mole on histology
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is reserved for women who have completed their family AND have a complete mole, or for refractory disease; suction curettage is the standard first-line for women wishing to preserve fertility (RANZCOG GTD).

options.text: Medical evacuation of the mole with methylergometrine and misoprostol, followed by weekly serum beta-hCG until undetectable for three consecutive weekly samples
options.rationale: Medical evacuation of a mole with uterotonics or prostaglandins is associated with higher rates of incomplete evacuation, embolisation of trophoblastic tissue and persistent GTN. Suction curettage is preferred (RANZCOG GTD).

stem: A 34-year-old G3P0 woman conceived through a single fresh embryo transfer IVF cycle two weeks ago and presents to a tertiary early pregnancy assessment service at 6 weeks and 5 days gestation by transfer date. She has had two previous tubal ectopic pregnancies managed with laparoscopic salpingectomy (left partial and right partial), with severe periadhesive disease documented at the second operation, and an extensive endometriosis history with bilateral chocolate cysts treated 4 years ago. She takes only luteal-phase progesterone pessaries 200 mg twice daily as part of her IVF protocol and a pregnancy multivitamin; she has no other medications and no drug allergies. Over the last 72 hours she has developed unilateral left iliac fossa pain that is intermittent but increasing, with one episode of dizziness this morning when standing. She has had no vaginal bleeding and is haemodynamically stable on arrival. Vital signs: BP 108/68 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.6 degC, SpO2 99%, respiratory rate 16. Abdominal examination reveals tenderness in the left iliac fossa without peritonism; the cervical os is closed and there is no active bleeding on speculum. Serum beta-hCG today is 12 000 IU/L. Transvaginal ultrasound by an experienced foetal medicine sonographer demonstrates a single intrauterine gestational sac containing a yolk sac and a viable foetal pole with CRL 7 mm and foetal cardiac activity at 122 bpm. There is a separate left adnexal complex mass measuring 30 mm with a hyperechoic ring (sometimes called the 'tubal ring' or 'bagel sign') and a small amount of free fluid in the pouch of Douglas (approximately 50 mL). Her LFTs, FBC, renal function and group/screen are normal; she is RhD positive. The on-call gynaecology consultant has been called and is en route.
lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Single-dose IM methotrexate 50 mg/m2 to treat the left adnexal ectopic, with serial beta-hCG monitoring to track resolution while continuing the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Methotrexate is contraindicated when a viable intrauterine pregnancy coexists with an ectopic (heterotopic pregnancy) because it would terminate the IUP; surgical management is required (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Expectant management with serial 48-hour beta-hCG and repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 1 week, given haemodynamic stability and the presence of a viable intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy with a 30 mm adnexal mass, increasing pain, dizziness and free fluid carries a high rupture risk; expectant management is not appropriate. The beta-hCG trajectory is also uninterpretable because the IUP confounds it (RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit for observation and analgesia with 6-hourly observations, planning to operate only if she develops haemodynamic compromise
options.rationale: Watchful waiting in a haemodynamically stable but symptomatic heterotopic pregnancy with adnexal mass and free fluid is unsafe; rupture risk is high and definitive surgery should be planned urgently rather than reactively (RANZCOG).

options.text: Ultrasound-guided potassium chloride injection into the adnexal mass with continued expectant care of the intrauterine pregnancy
options.rationale: KCl injection is occasionally used in interstitial or cornual ectopics where surgical access is hazardous, but it is not first-line for a tubal heterotopic with rupture risk and prior salpingectomy; laparoscopic surgery remains the standard (RANZCOG).

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic salpingectomy of the left adnexal ectopic with intra-operative care to preserve the intrauterine pregnancy, plus anti-D if RhD negative
options.rationale: Heterotopic pregnancy (coexistent IUP and ectopic, much more common after ART) requires surgical treatment of the ectopic component because methotrexate would terminate the IUP. Laparoscopic salpingectomy is preferred for a complete tubal ectopic, particularly with prior contralateral salpingectomy; care is taken to minimise uterine manipulation and continue luteal support to protect the IUP. Anti-D 250 IU IM is given if RhD negative as this is a sensitising event pre-12 weeks (RANZCOG; RCOG; NBA).

stem: A 25-year-old G1P0 at 40+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4100 g infant with a mediolateral episiotomy following a 90-minute active second stage. The CTG had been normal throughout and Apgar scores were 9 and 9. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities, GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative, and the antenatal course was uncomplicated apart from a borderline GTT that was normal on dietary management. Active management of the third stage has been completed with delivery of a complete placenta and intact membranes, with an estimated blood loss of 350 mL. On systematic per-vaginum and per-rectum examination by the obstetric registrar, there is a tear extending through the perineal skin, all layers of the perineal muscles, and approximately 60% of the thickness of the external anal sphincter complex. The internal anal sphincter is identified as intact on PR examination, and the rectal mucosa is intact with no buttonhole tear. Bleeding is controlled with pressure, the woman is haemodynamically stable, and she has a working epidural that has been topped up. The registrar plans repair in theatre with appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis and postoperative care.
lead_in: Which classification and repair plan is correct?

options.text: Second-degree perineal tear, repaired on the labour ward using absorbable suture material as standard
options.rationale: Second-degree tears involve perineal muscle but not the anal sphincter; involvement of the external anal sphincter makes this a third-degree (OASI) tear (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3a, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external anal sphincter repair
options.rationale: 3a is defined as <50% thickness of the external anal sphincter torn; this tear involves ~60% thickness, making it 3b (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3c, repair in theatre with a separate internal anal sphincter repair done
options.rationale: 3c requires internal anal sphincter involvement; the internal sphincter is intact on PR in this scenario (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

options.text: Third-degree tear grade 3b, repair in theatre with end-to-end or overlap external sphincter repair plus antibiotics, laxatives and physio
options.rationale: Sultan classification: 3a <50% EAS thickness, 3b >50% EAS thickness, 3c EAS and internal anal sphincter torn, 4 includes rectal mucosa. With ~60% EAS thickness and intact IAS and mucosa, this is a 3b. Repair in theatre under regional or general anaesthesia with end-to-end or overlap technique for the EAS (equivalent outcomes), single-dose IV antibiotics, oral laxatives (lactulose for 10-14 days), pelvic floor physiotherapy, and 6-12 week follow-up (RANZCOG OASI 2020; RCOG Green-top 29 The Management of Third- and Fourth-degree Perineal Tears).

options.text: Fourth-degree perineal tear, repaired in theatre with rectal mucosal closure as the first step
options.rationale: Fourth-degree tears extend through the rectal mucosa; the mucosa is intact here (RANZCOG OASI 2020).

stem: A 34-year-old G1P0 at 37+3 attends day assessment with a one-week history of frontal headaches not relieved by paracetamol and new BP elevation noted by her GP. Her booking BP was 110/68 and her booking BMI was 28, with no chronic hypertension, no renal disease, no autoimmune disease, no prior gestational hypertension and no family history of pre-eclampsia. She is a non-smoker and took aspirin 100 mg from 12 weeks. Today her BP on arrival is 158/102 on repeat measurement 15 minutes apart in the seated position with an appropriately sized cuff, urine protein creatinine ratio is 65 mg/mmol, platelets are 145 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, ALT 32 U/L, urate 0.39 mmol/L, and the foetus is appropriately grown on the 40th centile with normal umbilical artery Dopplers, normal AFI and a reassuring CTG. She has had labetalol 200 mg orally with a fall to 142/92 after one hour and the headache has eased. There is no eclampsia, no epigastric pain, no visual disturbance, no clonus, no pulmonary oedema and no oliguria. On vaginal examination the cervix is closed, posterior, 2 cm long, firm, station -3, giving a Bishop score of 1. The on-call consultant reviews her in the unit and discusses options regarding admission, antihypertensive management, MgSO4 use and timing and route of delivery.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home on oral labetalol with weekly clinic review until 39+0 then induce labour
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia diagnosed at or beyond 37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery in the same admission, not expectant management to 39 weeks (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Admit, continue oral antihypertensive to keep BP <140/90, induce labour this admission, MgSO4 only if severe features develop
options.rationale: Pre-eclampsia at >=37+0 weeks is an indication for delivery (SOMANZ 2023; NICE NG133; HYPITAT trial 2009). With a Bishop score of 1 the route is induction of labour rather than caesarean unless other obstetric indications. Antihypertensive target is <140/90 (oral labetalol, nifedipine or methyldopa). MgSO4 for seizure prophylaxis is indicated for severe pre-eclampsia (severe hypertension >=160/110, neurological features, severe biochemical features) rather than routine non-severe disease (SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023; NICE NG133).

options.text: Proceed to a category 2 caesarean section today given the unfavourable cervix on examination
options.rationale: Bishop 1 does not mandate caesarean in non-severe PE with reassuring foetal status; ripening with Foley or PGE2 followed by induction is appropriate (SOMANZ; RANZCOG).

options.text: Admit, give MgSO4 4 g loading then 1 g/h infusion regardless of severity, and induce labour now
options.rationale: Routine MgSO4 for all PE is not recommended in non-severe disease in SOMANZ; it is reserved for severe PE or eclampsia or imminent eclampsia features (severe BP, neurological signs, severe biochemistry) (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: Admit for 48 hours of betamethasone 11.4 mg IM x 2 doses before any induction
options.rationale: Antenatal corticosteroids are not indicated at 37+3 weeks because foetal lung maturity is sufficiently advanced; steroids are used between 24+0 and 34+6 (RANZCOG corticosteroids; SOMANZ).

stem: A 29-year-old G1P0 at 39+4 presents at 0200 with a sudden gush of clear fluid from the vagina that occurred 30 minutes ago while watching television. On sterile speculum examination there is a pool of clear amniotic fluid in the posterior fornix, with a positive fern test on a dried slide and a positive IGFBP-1 swab. There is no offensive odour, no PV bleeding, no meconium and no foetal scalp visible. The foetus is cephalic, the head is engaged on abdominal palpation, and the CTG is reassuring with a baseline of 138 bpm, good variability of 10-15 bpm, accelerations and no decelerations. The cervix is closed and 2 cm long on sterile speculum visualisation; digital examination has not been performed. She is afebrile at T 36.7, HR 78, BP 116/72 and her white cell count is 9 x10^9/L with CRP <5 mg/L. Her booking BMI was 24, she has no medical comorbidities and her GBS vaginorectal swab at 36+1 was negative. She is having occasional irregular tightenings every 10-20 minutes but is not in established labour. She would prefer to avoid medical interventions if possible and is keen on as physiological a birth as is safe. The on-call obstetric registrar discusses her options.
lead_in: Which best describes the optimal management plan for this woman?

options.text: Discharge home with daily review for up to 72 hours, then induce labour if not in labour
options.rationale: Australian practice for term PROM does not support prolonged outpatient expectant management beyond 24 hours; chorioamnionitis risk rises with time and induction within 24 h reduces this risk (Hannah TermPROM; RANZCOG; NICE NG235).

options.text: Offer immediate induction with oxytocin or expectant management to 24 hours, intrapartum GBS prophylaxis only if ROM >18 hours
options.rationale: The Hannah TermPROM trial (NEJM 1996) established that immediate IOL with oxytocin or expectant management for up to 96 hours have similar perinatal outcomes, but immediate IOL reduces chorioamnionitis and postpartum endometritis. Current Australian practice offers women either immediate IOL or expectant management with a clear plan to induce by approximately 24 hours from rupture, balancing maternal preference with infection risk. With negative GBS, intrapartum antibiotics are based on risk factors (ROM >18 h, intrapartum fever) rather than routine (Hannah ME et al NEJM 1996; RANZCOG Term Prelabour Rupture of Membranes; NICE NG235). Per RANZCOG term PROM guidance: induction at any time after PROM is acceptable; expectant management up to 24 hours is reasonable; GBS prophylaxis triggered by ROM >18 hours or other risk factors.

options.text: Commence empirical IV ampicillin plus gentamicin immediately and induce labour with vaginal prostaglandin gel today
options.rationale: Empirical antibiotics are not indicated in the absence of clinical infection or risk factors (GBS unknown/positive, prolonged ROM, fever); they would be added if she developed chorioamnionitis or ROM >18 h with negative GBS (Therapeutic Guidelines; RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform digital vaginal examination to assess Bishop score and plan immediate caesarean section for delivery
options.rationale: Digital vaginal examination after PROM should be deferred until labour is established or induction is planned because each exam increases infection risk; PROM is not an indication for caesarean (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence vaginal prostaglandin gel for cervical ripening overnight regardless of her stated preference now in admission
options.rationale: Patient preference matters and should be incorporated into the plan; immediate ripening is one option but not the sole appropriate plan (RANZCOG).

stem: A 32-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 has been pushing actively in the second stage for 110 minutes with an epidural providing adequate sensory block. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no medical comorbidities, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and her 36-week scan showed an appropriately grown foetus on the 55th centile with an EFW of 3500 g, normal Dopplers and normal liquor. The foetal head is at +2 station, in direct OA, with no caput and no moulding on examination by the registrar. The CTG has developed recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 8 minutes, and the registrar wishes to expedite delivery. The bladder has been catheterised and emptied, the maternal pelvis is clinically gynaecoid, and verbal consent for instrumental delivery in the room with theatre on standby has been obtained from the woman. Adequate epidural analgesia has been confirmed with cold testing, the paediatric team has been notified and the anaesthetist is in the room. The registrar applies Neville-Barnes forceps correctly with the operator's posterior wrist supported and performs three pulls with descent of the head from +2 to +3, and then no further descent on the fourth pull despite correct application, correct axis traction, and an effective maternal push with a contraction. The foetal head has not yet crowned and the perineum is not yet stretched. The consultant is now at the bedside and reviews the scenario before deciding the next step.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reapply forceps with a different cup angle and attempt two further pulls
options.rationale: Repeated forceps attempts after failure to descend with three correct pulls is associated with significant maternal and neonatal trauma and is not recommended; transition to caesarean is required (RANZCOG operative vaginal birth; RCOG Green-top 26).

options.text: Switch to a ventouse to complete delivery given the head is at +3
options.rationale: Sequential use of instruments (forceps then ventouse or vice versa) is associated with significantly increased neonatal morbidity (intracranial haemorrhage, subgaleal haemorrhage, cephalhaematoma) and should be avoided. Failed forceps mandates caesarean rather than a second instrument (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 26; ACOG).

options.text: Transfer immediately to theatre for category 1 caesarean section with senior consultant present
options.rationale: Failed forceps with pathological CTG mandates category 1 caesarean section. Sequential use of instruments is contraindicated due to combined neonatal trauma risk. The trial of instrumental should occur with theatre on standby precisely so this conversion can occur within minutes. The senior consultant should be present given the complexity (RANZCOG Instrumental Vaginal Birth 2016; RCOG Green-top 26 Operative Vaginal Birth; NICE NG235).

options.text: Ask the mother to push without the forceps for one more contraction
options.rationale: The CTG is pathological and pushing without instrument has already failed for >100 minutes; expediting delivery in theatre is required (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a symphysiotomy at the bedside to widen the pelvis for delivery
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is reserved for cases of shoulder dystocia or where caesarean is impossible (low-resource settings) and is not appropriate here (RANZCOG; WHO).

stem: A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+5 is in spontaneous labour. Twin 1 is cephalic and growth-appropriate on the 45th centile, twin 2 has been transverse throughout the third trimester on serial scans with the back inferior and is 200 g smaller than twin 1 (35th centile, within the 20% inter-twin discordance threshold and with no growth discordance suggestive of selective FGR). She had a previous uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal birth at 39+1 of a singleton with a normal third stage and no perineal trauma beyond a second-degree tear. There is no previous caesarean, no uterine surgery and no medical comorbidities. Her booking BMI was 26, the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative and she has taken iron supplementation throughout the pregnancy with a current haemoglobin of 118 g/L. Both foetuses have reassuring CTGs on dual monitoring. The cervix is 6 cm, station of the leading twin is -1, membranes are intact for the leading twin and ARM has not been performed, and an experienced obstetric consultant with extensive twin delivery experience is available throughout the labour. The anaesthetist is in the unit and an epidural has been sited and is providing good analgesia. She has expressed a clear preference for a vaginal birth if safe. After 4 hours she is fully dilated, twin 1 head is at +3, and she pushes for 25 minutes to deliver a 2900 g cephalic infant with normal Apgars. On bedside rescan by the consultant immediately after delivery of twin 1, twin 2 remains transverse with the back inferior. The CTG of twin 2 is reassuring at this point, membranes of twin 2 are intact, and the consultant is at the bedside in the delivery suite with a theatre and a paediatric team on standby.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step for delivery of twin 2?

options.text: Internal podalic version followed by breech extraction of twin 2
options.rationale: With twin 2 transverse after delivery of twin 1 and an experienced operator available, internal podalic version (operator inserts a hand through the cervix, identifies a foetal foot, brings it down through the introitus) followed by breech extraction is the preferred approach in Australia and is supported by the Twin Birth Study (Barrett JF et al NEJM 2013). External cephalic version is an alternative but has lower success after delivery of the first twin and risks foetal distress and abruption. Combined vaginal-CS delivery has worse outcomes than planned CS. The reassuring CTG provides a window for intervention, and internal podalic version with breech extraction is the most reliable mode in experienced hands. CS should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or non-reassuring CTG (RANZCOG Management of Twin Pregnancy 2022; RCOG Green-top 51 Twin and Triplet Pregnancy).

options.text: External cephalic version under ultrasound, then expect spontaneous cephalic delivery
options.rationale: ECV after delivery of twin 1 is an option but has lower success rates than internal podalic version (~60% vs >90% in experienced hands), and any delay risks cord prolapse, abruption or cervical closure. ECV may be tried first by some operators but internal podalic version + breech extraction is more reliable when the operator is experienced (RANZCOG; Twin Birth Study).

options.text: Category 2 caesarean section for delivery of twin 2 now
options.rationale: Combined vaginal-caesarean delivery (vaginal twin 1, CS twin 2) is associated with worse outcomes for both mother and twin 2 and should be reserved for failed manoeuvres or foetal compromise; the Twin Birth Study demonstrated that planned vaginal birth in a centre with experienced operators is safe (Barrett NEJM 2013).

options.text: Await spontaneous descent of twin 2 for up to 60 minutes before any intervention
options.rationale: Twin 2 transverse will not descend spontaneously; the inter-twin interval should be as short as practical (commonly within 30 minutes) before intervention to avoid cervical closure, cord prolapse with subsequent ARM, and abruption (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform ARM of twin 2 membranes immediately to allow descent
options.rationale: ARM of twin 2 while transverse risks cord prolapse and limb presentation; membranes should be left intact until the presenting part is engaged after version or breech extraction (RANZCOG; RCOG).

stem: A 32-year-old G0 presents with 14 months of constant pelvic pain rated 5/10 at baseline and 8/10 cyclically. The pain has both cyclical features (worse in the 4 days prior to and during her period) and non-cyclical features (dull background ache present every day for the last 8 months). She reports dyspareunia on deep penetration, dyschezia during menses, and increased urinary frequency without dysuria or haematuria. She is sexually active in a stable monogamous relationship of 6 years with no STI history, condom use is inconsistent, and last STI screen was 14 months ago and negative. She has been trying to conceive for 9 months without success. There is no fever, no postmenopausal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding, no unexplained weight loss, no PR bleeding, and no haematuria. Her mother had endometriosis diagnosed at laparoscopy. On bimanual examination there is uterosacral nodularity and a fixed retroverted uterus with cervical excitation. A pelvic ultrasound 2 months ago showed a 3 cm left ovarian endometrioma with ground-glass appearance and normal uterine cavity. STI screen today is pending. She works as a teacher and reports anxiety and disrupted sleep due to the pain.
lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure that endometriosis is confirmed on ultrasound and commence the COCP continuously without further investigation
options.rationale: Although the clinical picture and imaging are consistent with endometriosis, fertility goals, deep endometriosis on examination (uterosacral nodularity, fixed retroversion), and pending STI screen require an integrated specialist assessment; commencing the COCP would suppress fertility in someone trying to conceive (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Insert a 52 mg levonorgestrel IUD now and review symptoms in 6 months
options.rationale: LNG-IUS is effective for endometriosis-related pain but is contraceptive and inappropriate for a woman actively trying to conceive; fertility goals must guide therapy (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Start a GnRH agonist with add-back tibolone for 6 months and reassess symptoms
options.rationale: GnRH agonists are second-line medical therapy reserved for refractory endometriosis after first-line failure; they are contraceptive and would delay fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022).

options.text: Proceed directly to diagnostic laparoscopy without prior imaging or specialist multidisciplinary review at all
options.rationale: Laparoscopy is no longer required to diagnose endometriosis when imaging and clinical features are characteristic; preoperative MRI for deep disease mapping and multidisciplinary planning improves surgical outcomes and protects fertility (ESHRE Endometriosis 2022; RANZCOG).

options.text: Refer to a multidisciplinary endometriosis service for specialist assessment, MRI for deep infiltrating disease mapping, and fertility-focused management
options.rationale: Suspected deep infiltrating endometriosis (uterosacral nodularity, dyschezia, fixed retroversion) with an endometrioma and subfertility warrants MRI mapping and multidisciplinary specialist input combining gynaecology, fertility, pelvic pain physiotherapy and psychology; the Australian National Action Plan for Endometriosis recommends this pathway (RANZCOG Endometriosis Statement; National Action Plan for Endometriosis; Pelvic Pain Foundation Australia).
